headline: FIFA Rejects Egypt's Bias Claim
deck: Refereeing chief Pierluigi Collina defended the disputed calls in Argentina's win over Egypt and said the officials answer to no one, the FIFA president included. The bracket moved on regardless: France blanked Morocco 2-0 to become the first team into the semi-finals.
body: FIFA answered Egypt's protest with a defense rather than a review. Refereeing chief Pierluigi Collina publicly backed the officiating in Argentina's 3-2 round-of-16 win, saying VAR correctly overturned Mostafa Zico's goal after spotting a foul by Marwan Attia on Lisandro Martinez in the build-up, and that the uncalled contact on Mohamed Salah before Argentina's winner was "normal football contact" [E1]. He rejected any suggestion of favouritism, insisting nobody "can claim that FIFA refereeing can be influenced by anyone, not even by the FIFA president" [E1].

Egypt had asked for more than an explanation. The Egyptian Football Association said it "cannot remain silent" over what it called unfair and biased officiating, objecting to the disallowed goal and the non-call moments before the decisive move, and some voices pressed for the referee to be sent home [E1]. What the governing body offered instead was a point-by-point defense of the crew and a statement of its independence [E1].

On the field the tournament kept its own schedule. France beat Morocco 2-0 at Gillette Stadium to become the first team through to the semi-finals, where it will meet the winner of Belgium and Spain in Dallas on 14 July [E2]. Belgium and Spain met later in the quarter-final round, with Argentina drawing Switzerland and Norway facing England on 11 July [E2].

The institutional move is the story under the scores. A complaint met with a defense, not an inquiry, is how a federation closes a dispute rather than reopening it, and the record now shows FIFA standing behind its officials instead of revisiting them [E1]. Whether that answer settles Egypt's anger or hardens it is the part still in play [E1].

The limits are worth stating. Collina's defense is not proof the calls were right, only that FIFA will not reconsider them, and Egypt's grievance is not evidence of corruption [E1]. The discriminator is procedural, whether any formal disciplinary step follows, of which there is none so far, or whether the matter simply recedes as the bracket advances toward Dallas. For now the calls stand, and France plays on [E1][E2].

evidence_box.fragment: FIFA refereeing chief Pierluigi Collina defended the Argentina-Egypt officiating, said VAR correctly overturned Zico's goal and the Salah contact was normal, and that refereeing cannot be influenced by anyone, not even the FIFA president
France beat Morocco 2-0 to reach the semi-finals as the first team through; semi-final vs Belgium/Spain on 14 July; Argentina-Switzerland and Norway-England on 11 July
